satisfies the four aforementioned conditions, if they turned out to be non-associative or
non-distributive, then this set can never be a field.
Associativity of ’+’
To verify associativity of ’+’, we need to verify that ∀x, y, z ∈
F
(x + y) + z = x + (y + z). To do this exhaustively, we will have to go through 33 = 27
combinations, but we can think carefully and leverage the other verified properties to
minimize the verification effort:
i. If any of them is = 0, then the property is satisfied for all combinations of the other
two.
ii. We restrict our attention to x, y, z ∈{1, a}.
So we now have to consider only 8
combinations. if x = y = z, then the property is satisfied. Then we don’t have to
consider the cases where x = y = z = a or x = y = z = 1.
iii. You can easily show that the remaining 6 combinations satisfy the property.
Therefore, ’+’ is associative.
Associativity of ’.’
Is (x.y).z = x.(y.z) ∀x, y, z ∈F? We can reduce the number of
verifications by noticing that if any variable is = 0, both sides = 0. If any variable = 1,
then the property holds for all the other values of the other two variables. We are only left
with the case of x = y = z = a, where the property is satisfied. Then ’.’ is associative.

Distributivity
Is x.(y + z) = x.y + x.z ∀x, y, z ∈F?
i. If x = 0: LHS = 0.(y + z) = 0, RHS = 0.y + 0.z = 0 + 0 = 0, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
ii. If x = 1: LHS = 1.(y + z) = y + z, RHS = 1.y + 1.z = y + z, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
iii. If x = a: If any of y or z = 0, the property is satisfied for all the values of the
other one: x.(0 + z) = x.z, and x.0 + x.z = 0 + x.z = x.z. x.(y + 0) = x.y, and
x.y + x.0 = x.y + 0 = x.u. We are only left with four cases (3 only if you notice that
by commutativity x.(y + z) = x.(z + y), and x.y + x.z = x.z + x.y).
A. a.(1 + 1) = a.a = 1, and a.1 + a.1 = a + a = 1.
B. a.(a + a) = a.1 = a, and a.a + a.a = 1 + 1 = a.
C. a.(1 + a) = a.0 = 0, and a.1 + a.a = a + 0.
Then distributivity is satisfied. Therefore F is a field equipped with ’+’ and ’.’ as defined
above.
Note
Let a = 2, then the addition becomes the real numbers addition modulo 3 and
the multiplication becomes the real multiplication module 3. This is the Galois field of 3
(GF(3)).

Using the results from part (a), solve for x ∈F such that
i. a.x + 1 = a.
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a.

title: Field Operations
text:
Now, Let’s solve for x ∈F such that:
i. a.x + 1 = a: This is analogous to linear equations over R (try graphing it). Since
this is a finite field, we can solve this using two methods, namely, exhaustive search,
where ewe plug in values for x and check if the equality is satisfied, or deal with it
algebraically as we used to do in high school. We will opt here for the second method.
Add the additive inverse of 1, namely a, from the right:
(a.x + 1) + a = a + a
LHS = a.x + (1 + a)
(Associativity of ’+’)
= a.x + 0 = a.x
RHS = a + a = 1
∴a.x = 1
Then x is the multiplicative inverse of a, namely x = a (multiply by the multiplicative
inverse of a from the left and apply associativity).
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a: The first step is similar to the previous equation, we add the aditive
inverse of 1 form the right and use associativity, then a.x.x = 1. Now we multiply
with the multiplicative inverse of a from the left and apply associativity. Therefore
x.x = a. The values for x.x are on the diagonal of the ’.’ table. Obviously no element
in F when multiplied by itself becomes = a. There is no solution for x ∈F.
Recall that x2 = −1 has no solution in R, although it has two solutions in C, namely
± j.

The set = {[x1, x2, x3, x4] ∈R4 |x3 = x1 + x2, x4 = x1 −x2} is a subspace. First not that
this set is non-empty and [0, 0, 0, 0] ∈S. Let x, y ∈S, then
z = x + y = [x1 + y1, x2 + y2, x3 + y3, x4 + y4]
we have
z3 = x3 + y3 = (x1 + x2) + (y1 + y2) = (x1 + y1) + (x2 + y2) = z1 + z2
z4 = x4 + y4 = (x1 −x2) + (y1 −y2) = (x1 + y1) −(x2 + y2) = z1 −z2
Thus S is closed under vector addition. Let x ∈S, and α ∈R, then
y = αx = [αx1, αx2, αx3, αx4]
we have,
y3 = αx3 = α(x1 + x2) = αx1 + αx2 = y1 + y2
y4 = αx4 = α(x1 −x2) = αx1 −αx2 = y1 −y2
Hence S is also closed under scalar multiplication and it is a subspace.

we have
z3 = x3 + y3 = (x1 + x2) + (y1 + y2) = (x1 + y1) + (x2 + y2) = z1 + z2
z4 = x4 + y4 = (x1 −x2) + (y1 −y2) = (x1 + y1) −(x2 + y2) = z1 −z2
Thus S is closed under vector addition. Let x ∈S, and α ∈R, then
y = αx = [αx1, αx2, αx3, αx4]
we have,
y3 = αx3 = α(x1 + x2) = αx1 + αx2 = y1 + y2
y4 = αx4 = α(x1 −x2) = αx1 −αx2 = y1 −y2
Hence S is also closed under scalar multiplication and it is a subspace.

The set S = {B ∈Rn×n | AB = BA} is a subspace. First note that the set is non-empty
as A ∈S. Let B1, B2 ∈S, Then
A(B1 + B2) = AB1 + AB2 = B1A + B2A = (B1 + B2)A
hence (B1 + B2) ∈S and let α ∈R
A(αB1) = αAB1 = αB1A = (αB1)A
hence αB1 ∈S. Since S is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, it is a subspace.

title: Affine functions
text: Suppose that A ∈Rm×n and b ∈Rm. Show that the function f (x) = Ax + b is affine.

title: Affine functions
text:
Let x, y ∈Rn and α, β ∈R with α + β = 1
f(αx + βy) = A(αx + βy) + b
= A(αx) + A(βy) + (α + β)b
= αAx + βAy + αb + βb
= α(Ax + b) + β(Ay + b)
= αf(x) + βf(y)
Hence f(x) is affine.

title: Affine functions

Hence, f(x) = Ax + b for some unique A ∈Rm×n and b ∈Rm.

text:
Suppose F = R. Either prove that rank(AAT)=rank(A) or provide a counterexam-
ple.

title: Rank of AAT
text:
F = R : Let x ∈N(A⊤), then A⊤x = 0, and AA⊤x = 0, therefore x ∈N(AA⊤).
Thus, N(A⊤) ⊆N(AA⊤), and dim(N(A⊤)) ≤dim(N(AA⊤)). By rank-nullity
theorem we have
rank(AA⊤) ≤rank(A⊤).
(1)
Let x ∈N(AA⊤), then AA⊤x = 0, and x⊤AA⊤x = ⟨A⊤x, A⊤x⟩= 0 where
⟨a, b⟩= b⊤a is a valid inner product of Rn over the field R. By the properties of the

inner product, this occurs iff A⊤x = 0, i.e., x ∈N(A⊤). Thus, N(AA⊤) ⊆N(A⊤),
and dim(N(AA⊤)) ≤dim(N(A⊤)). By rank-nullity theorem we have
rank(A⊤) ≤rank(AA⊤).
(2)
From (1) and (2), we have rank(A⊤) = rank(AA⊤).
Since rank(A⊤) = rank(A), then rank(AA⊤) = rank(A).

A ∈Fm×n, B ∈Fn×k
The range space of B denoted R(B) is a subspace of Fn.
Restrict the linear map A to R(B) i.e. consider new linear map f : R(B) →Fm
given by f(x) = Ax for all vectors x in R(B).
Domain of f is R(B) and hence dimension of domain is rank(B).
R(f) = {Ay : y ∈R(B)} = {ABx : x ∈Fk} = R(AB)
N(f) = {y ∈R(B) : Ay = 0} = N(A) ∩R(B)
We can apply Rank-Nullity Theorem on this new map f.
rank(B) = dim(R(f)) + dim(N(f))
= rank(AB) + dim(N(A) ∩R(B))
≤rank(AB) + dim(N(A))

It suffices to show that ∥x −v∥is minimized over all v ∈R(P) by v∗= Px. For
any v ∈R(P):
∥x −v∥2 = ∥x −Px + Px −v∥2 = ∥x −Px∥2 + ∥Px −v∥2 + 2(x −Px)⊤(Px −v)
= ∥x −Px∥2 + ∥Px −v∥2 + 2(x⊤−x⊤P)(Px −v)
= ∥x −Px∥2 + ∥Px −v∥2 + 2(x⊤Px −x⊤v −x⊤P2x + x⊤Pv)
= ∥x −Px∥2 + ∥Px −v∥2 + 2(x⊤Px −x⊤v −x⊤Px + x⊤v)
= ∥x −Px∥2 + ∥Px −v∥2 ≥∥x −Px∥2.
Where we used P = P2 and Pv = v, ∀v ∈R(P)(v = Px = P2x = Pv ). To
achieve equality in the last inequality, we need ∥Px −v∥= 0 →v = Px. Thus,
arg minv∈R(P) ∥x −v∥= Px.

text:

title: Vector Space Definition
text: A vector space V over a field F is a set equipped with two operations: vector addition and scalar multiplication. These operations must satisfy the following properties: closure, associativity, commutativity of addition, existence of an additive identity, existence of additive inverses, distributive properties, and compatibility with scalar multiplication. Examples include R^n, the space of polynomials, and the space of continuous functions.

title: Linear Independence
text: A set of vectors {v1, v2, ..., vk} in a vector space V is said to be linearly independent if the equation a1 v1 + a2 v2 + ... + ak vk = 0 implies that a1 = a2 = ... = ak = 0. If there exist non-zero coefficients such that the linear combination equals the zero vector, the vectors are linearly dependent.

title: Span of Vectors
text: The span of a set of vectors {v1, v2, ..., vk} is the set of all linear combinations of these vectors. Formally, span{v1,...,vk} = {a1 v1 + a2 v2 + ... + ak vk | a1,...,ak ∈ F}. The span represents the smallest subspace that contains all of the vectors.

title: Matrix Rank
text: The rank of a matrix A is the dimension of its column space, which is the span of the columns of A. Equivalently, the rank is the number of pivot columns obtained after performing Gaussian elimination on the matrix. The rank indicates the maximum number of linearly independent columns (or rows) in the matrix.

title: Null Space
text: The null space of a matrix A, denoted N(A), is the set of all vectors x such that Ax = 0. It represents the solution space of the homogeneous system associated with A. The null space is always a subspace of R^n where n is the number of columns of A.

title: Rank Nullity Theorem
text: The Rank-Nullity Theorem states that for an m×n matrix A, the dimension of the column space plus the dimension of the null space equals the number of columns of A. Formally, rank(A) + nullity(A) = n. This theorem connects the number of independent constraints in a system with the dimension of its solution space.

title: Basis of a Vector Space
text: A basis of a vector space V is a set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans the space V. Every vector in V can be written uniquely as a linear combination of the basis vectors. The number of vectors in any basis of V is called the dimension of V.

title: Dimension of a Vector Space
text: The dimension of a vector space V is defined as the number of vectors in any basis of V. It represents the number of degrees of freedom or the minimum number of coordinates needed to specify any vector in the space. For example, R^n has dimension n.
